name: 1P/Halley
description: The most famous comet, returning every 75–76 years. Its 1986 visit was met by a fleet of spacecraft; it returns in 2061. Its orbit is retrograde — it circles the Sun backwards.
facts: Recorded by astronomers since at least 240 BC.
Halley's dust causes two meteor showers every year: the Eta Aquariids and the Orionids.

name: 2P/Encke
description: The comet with the shortest known orbital period: just 3.3 years. Its debris stream produces the Taurid meteor showers.
facts: Encke has been observed on more returns than any other comet.

name: 67P/Churyumov–Gerasimenko
description: The rubber-duck-shaped comet orbited for two years by ESA's Rosetta spacecraft, which landed the Philae probe on it in 2014 — the first comet landing in history.
facts: Rosetta revealed the comet is two lobes gently fused together, softer than packed snow in places.

name: C/1995 O1 (Hale–Bopp)
description: The Great Comet of 1997, visible to the naked eye for a record 18 months. With a nucleus around 60 km across, it is one of the largest comets ever observed.
facts: Hale–Bopp won't return until roughly the year 4385.
Its orbit is tilted almost 90° to the plane of the planets — look for it far above the ecliptic.

name: 109P/Swift–Tuttle
description: The parent of the Perseid meteor shower: every August, Earth ploughs through the dust this comet leaves behind. It returns in 2126.
facts: At 26 km across, it is the largest known object that repeatedly passes close to Earth.
